summary:
Bartholomew Dias's rounding of the Cape of Good Hope (February 1488 AD) was the crucial breakthrough in [[event:3054|Portuguese exploration of Africa]] that demonstrated the sea route to India was navigable — that the Indian Ocean was not a landlocked sea (as some ancient geographers had theorized) but connected to the Atlantic via Africa's southern tip. Dias commanded a fleet of two caravels and a supply ship, pushed further south than any previous European expedition, weathered a severe storm that drove him south of the cape without seeing it, and emerged to find the African coastline running eastward rather than southward — the definitive proof that the continent had an end.

Dias rounded the cape itself (which he named 'Cape of Storms' for the violent weather he encountered — King João II renamed it 'Cape of Good Hope' for the hope it offered of reaching India) and sailed east and northeast along the South African coast as far as Kwaaihoek (Fish River), where his crew refused to go further. Dias erected a padrão (stone pillar asserting Portuguese sovereignty) and turned back. His voyage took 16 months and established the navigational knowledge — the seasonal wind patterns, the ocean currents, the geography of southern Africa — that [[event:2290|Vasco da Gama]] would use a decade later to complete the journey to India. Dias himself later participated in da Gama's India expedition as far as the Cape Verde Islands and served on Pedro Álvares Cabral's 1500 fleet (which accidentally discovered Brazil); he drowned in a storm off the Cape of Good Hope — the cape he had first rounded — in 1500.
